items.correct: Learning by observing the consequences others receive for behaviour.
items.misconception: Being directly rewarded for copying someone.
items.why: Students confuse vicarious with direct reinforcement. 'Vicarious' means experienced through someone else - you see them rewarded/punished and adjust your own behaviour accordingly.

items.q: Which part of the psyche operates on the reality principle?
items.why: Students confuse ego (reality) with superego (morality). The ego mediates between id's demands and reality's constraints. The superego is the internalised moral conscience.

items.q: What are the three elements of Beck's negative triad?
items.correct: Negative views of self, world, and future.
items.misconception: Negative views of past, present, and future.
items.why: Students often substitute 'past' for 'self' or 'world'. The triad is about the DOMAINS of negative thought (self/world/future), not time periods.
The answer examiners want is: Strong negative. “Weak negative” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An in-depth study of one individual is a:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Case study. “Survey” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Maximum value of a Pearson correlation coefficient:
items.why: Exact answer: 1. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
